title: ASEAN’s critical minerals and industrial-upgrade dilemma
gist: ASEAN wants to move from raw critical-mineral exporter to high-value processor and manufacturer, but China's near-monopoly on refining, heavy financing requirements, and intra-bloc competition mean the pivot could deepen external dependence rather than reduce it.

insights.claim: Indonesia accounts for over 60% of the global nickel supply, making the country a structural choke point for battery and EV supply chains.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Any disruption or policy shift in Indonesian nickel ripples through global battery cost curves; watch Indonesian export and processing policy as a price driver.
insights.quote: Indonesia accounts for over 60% of the global nickel supply.

insights.claim: The Philippines is the world's largest nickel-ore exporter and holds an estimated US$170 billion in nickel deposits.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: The Philippines is a second concentrated node in nickel supply; its export and downstream policy is a watch item alongside Indonesia.
insights.quote: The Philippines is the world’s largest nickel-ore exporter and possesses an estimated US$170 billion in nickel deposits.
insights.locator: Opening paragraph, 'The Philippines is the world’s largest'

insights.claim: Within ASEAN, nickel, cobalt and copper production is concentrated in Indonesia and the Philippines, creating high exposure to market shocks and geopolitical disruption across the whole bloc.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Geographic concentration means a single-country shock cascades regionally; model ASEAN mineral supply as two-country risk, not diversified.
insights.locator: Industrial upgrading section, 'Within ASEAN, high geographical concentration'

insights.claim: China controls more than 90% of global rare-earth-element refining capacity and 60-70% of lithium and cobalt refining, so ASEAN's move downstream may increase rather than reduce its dependence on China.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Downstream ambition without independent refining capacity hands China leverage; the bottleneck to watch is midstream processing, not mine output.
insights.quote: China accounts for more than 90% of global REEs-refining capacity, and 60–70% for lithium and cobalt.
insights.locator: Industrial upgrading section, 'Moving up the value chain'

insights.claim: Chinese firms control around 75% of Indonesia's nickel-refining capacity, embedding Chinese ownership at the core of ASEAN's flagship downstream sector.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Indonesian nickel 'downstreaming' is largely China-financed; Western de-risking strategies that ignore this ownership layer will underestimate the lock-in.
insights.quote: In Indonesia, Chinese firms control around 75% of the country’s nickel-refining capacity, including via major investments in industrial complexes.
insights.locator: Financing constraints section, 'several key midstream processing capacities'

themes.theme: Financing and the West's late entry

insights.claim: Developing new mines and processing facilities involves long lead times and substantial long-term capital, so ASEAN cannot respond quickly to demand surges or supply cuts.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Capacity is slow to build regardless of policy intent; treat ASEAN downstream capacity as a multi-year, not near-term, supply story.
insights.locator: Financing constraints section, 'Developing new mines and processing facilities'

insights.claim: Multilateral and bilateral financing for ASEAN minerals is accelerating, with the Asian Development Bank launching new supply-chain financing tools in May 2026 and Japan plus the World Bank expanding resource-resilience initiatives through 2026.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Public capital is mobilising to counter China; watch ADB, Japan and World Bank facilities as the funding channel for non-Chinese ASEAN processing.
insights.locator: Financing constraints section, 'In May 2026, the Asian Development Bank'

insights.claim: US critical-mineral deals with ASEAN states are still early-stage and will take years to mobilise capital and build equivalent influence to China's, leaving a structural vulnerability in the interim.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: The China-to-West transition in ASEAN processing is a slow burn; near-term supply chains remain China-dependent despite the headlines on US deals.
insights.quote: It will take time to mobilise capital, build infrastructure and supply chains and establish an equivalent regional influence to that of China.
insights.locator: Financing constraints section, 'While the United States has just signed deals'

insights.claim: Following Pax Silica (December 2025) and the US Critical Minerals Ministerial (February 2026), Australia, India, Japan, Singapore and South Korea have become core participants in US-led critical-mineral alliances, with bilateral US deals signed with Indonesia, Malaysia and Thailand.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: A US-anchored minerals bloc is forming around Indo-Pacific middle powers; positioning follows these alliance lines for non-China supply diversification.
insights.locator: Regional economic growth and competition section, 'states around the world have become more explicitly involved'

insights.claim: US-led mineral frameworks may intensify geo-economic fragmentation within ASEAN as some members align with Washington while others keep prioritising ties with China, eroding bloc cohesion.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: ASEAN is unlikely to act as a unified mineral bloc; expect divergent national policies, complicating any pan-ASEAN supply-chain thesis.
insights.quote: these developments may also intensify geo-economic fragmentation within ASEAN
insights.locator: Regional economic growth and competition section, 'On the other hand'

insights.claim: Compared with Africa and Latin America, ASEAN offers Asian partners geographic proximity to Asia's manufacturing supply chain plus growing economies, a structural advantage in supplier selection.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Proximity-to-manufacturing favours ASEAN over other resource regions for Asian buyers; a tailwind for ASEAN integration into regional supply chains.
insights.locator: Regional economic growth and competition section, 'compared with other resource-rich regions'

themes.theme: Demand drivers and the core dilemma

insights.claim: Global critical-mineral demand is set to rise further as renewable-energy adoption, AI spread, data centres and rearmament all increase consumption simultaneously.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Multiple secular demand engines compound; structurally bullish backdrop for critical-mineral suppliers over the cycle.
insights.locator: Complex yet promising section, 'global demand for critical minerals is likely to further increase'

insights.claim: Whether ASEAN's mineral wealth becomes a foundation for industrial transformation or a new form of dependence will depend on who finances, processes and integrates the region into next-generation supply chains.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: The decisive variable is control of financing and processing, not mineral endowment; track which power funds and refines ASEAN output.
insights.quote: Whether ASEAN’s mineral wealth becomes a foundation for long-term industrial transformation or a new form of dependence may ultimately depend on who finances, processes and integrates the region
insights.locator: Closing section, 'The structure of contemporary critical-mineral-supply chains'
